title: Hopea cordifolia
intro: Hopea cordifolia, also known as the heart-shaped ironwood or heart-leaved ironwood, is a species of tree in the family Dipterocarpaceae, native to Southeast Asia. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues, due to its anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Hopea cordifolia include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often used to make teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into topical creams and ointments.
taxonomy: Hopea cordifolia is a species of tree in the family Dipterocarpaceae. It is native to tropical Asia and belongs to the genus Hopea. The genus Hopea is a group of trees in the family Dipterocarpaceae, commonly known as the dipterocarp family. The family Dipterocarpaceae is a group of flowering plants in the order Malvales. This classification places Hopea cordifolia within the broader plant kingdom.
uses: Hopea cordifolia is known for its medicinal uses, particularly in traditional Southeast Asian medicine. Its bark and leaves are used to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin infections. The plant is also believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which contribute to its medicinal value. In traditional medicine, Hopea cordifolia is used to treat digestive issues and to promote wound healing. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's potential medicinal applications.
